examples.title: Algorithm: Memory Allocation Strategies
examples.description: Demonstrates First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit, and Next-Fit memory allocation algorithms
examples.code:
ALGORITHM MemoryAllocation
INPUT: Memory holes list, Process size, Allocation strategy
OUTPUT: Allocated memory block or failure message

// Data Structures
STRUCT Hole
 start_address, size, process_id (default: NULL)
ENDSTRUCT

STRUCT MemoryManager
 holes[] // List of holes
 total_memory
ENDSTRUCT

// ===== FIRST-FIT ALGORITHM =====
FUNCTION FirstFit(memory_manager, process_size)
 FOR each hole IN memory_manager.holes
 IF hole.size >= process_size AND hole.process_id == NULL THEN
 ALLOCATE hole TO process_size
 RETURN success with allocation details
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN failure ("No suitable hole found")
END FUNCTION

// ===== BEST-FIT ALGORITHM =====
FUNCTION BestFit(memory_manager, process_size)
 best_hole ← NULL
 min_size ← INFINITY
 
 FOR each hole IN memory_manager.holes
 IF hole.size >= process_size AND hole.process_id == NULL THEN
 IF hole.size < min_size THEN
 min_size ← hole.size
 best_hole ← hole
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 IF best_hole ≠ NULL THEN
 ALLOCATE best_hole TO process_size
 RETURN success
 END IF
 RETURN failure
END FUNCTION

// ===== WORST-FIT ALGORITHM =====
FUNCTION WorstFit(memory_manager, process_size)
 worst_hole ← NULL
 max_size ← -1
 
 FOR each hole IN memory_manager.holes
 IF hole.size >= process_size AND hole.process_id == NULL THEN
 IF hole.size > max_size THEN
 max_size ← hole.size
 worst_hole ← hole
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 IF worst_hole ≠ NULL THEN
 ALLOCATE worst_hole TO process_size
 RETURN success
 END IF
 RETURN failure
END FUNCTION

// ===== NEXT-FIT ALGORITHM =====
FUNCTION NextFit(memory_manager, process_size, last_index)
 n ← length(memory_manager.holes)
 start_index ← (last_index + 1) MOD n
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO n-1
 index ← (start_index + i) MOD n
 hole ← memory_manager.holes[index]
 
 IF hole.size >= process_size AND hole.process_id == NULL THEN
 ALLOCATE hole TO process_size
 last_index ← index
 RETURN success with updated last_index
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN failure
END FUNCTION

// ===== ALLOCATION HELPER =====
FUNCTION ALLOCATE(hole, process_size)
 IF hole.size > process_size THEN
 // Create new hole for remaining memory
 new_hole.start_address ← hole.start_address + process_size
 new_hole.size ← hole.size - process_size
 new_hole.process_id ← NULL
 INSERT new_hole INTO holes list after current hole
 END IF
 
 hole.size ← process_size
 hole.process_id ← allocated_process_id
END FUNCTION

// ===== MEMORY COMPACTION (External Fragmentation Solution) =====
FUNCTION CompactMemory(memory_manager)
 // Sort holes by start address and consolidate free blocks
 SORT memory_manager.holes BY start_address
 consolidated_holes ← []
 
 FOR each hole IN memory_manager.holes
 IF hole.process_id ≠ NULL THEN
 // Move process to consolidated position
 new_address ← calculate_next_available_address()
 MOVE_PROCESS(hole.process_id, new_address)
 ADD hole TO consolidated_holes
 ELSE
 // Merge with previous free hole if exists
 IF last_hole IN consolidated_holes AND last_hole.process_id == NULL THEN
 last_hole.size ← last_hole.size + hole.size
 ELSE
 ADD hole TO consolidated_holes
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 memory_manager.holes ← consolidated_holes
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates four fundamental memory allocation algorithms: First-Fit finds the first available hole large enough, Best-Fit searches for the smallest suitable hole to minimize wasted space, Worst-Fit uses the largest hole to leave bigger free fragments, and Next-Fit continues searching from where the last allocation ended. The ALLOCATE helper function handles splitting holes when the allocated memory is smaller than the hole size. Memory compaction is also shown as a solution to external fragmentation.

examples.title: Python: Memory Allocation Simulation
examples.description: Implements memory allocation algorithms with fragmentation tracking and visualization
examples.explanation: This Python implementation provides a complete memory allocation simulation with First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit, and Next-Fit algorithms. It includes MemoryBlock class for representing memory segments, MemoryManager class for handling allocations, and methods for deallocation with adjacent block merging. The get_statistics() method calculates fragmentation metrics, and display() visualizes the memory map. The demonstration shows allocation, deallocation, and the resulting external fragmentation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for First-Fit and Next-Fit, O(n) for Best-Fit and Worst-Fit

examples.title: C: Memory Protection with Base and Limit Registers
examples.description: Demonstrates memory protection using base and limit registers with address translation
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates memory protection using base and limit registers, which is a fundamental OS memory protection mechanism. The translate_address() function performs bounds checking before translating logical addresses to physical addresses. If a process attempts to access memory beyond its allocated limit, a protection violation is triggered. The context_switch() function simulates how the OS loads different base/limit values when switching between processes, ensuring each process can only access its own memory region. This mirrors how the Memory Management Unit (MMU) works in real systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for address translation and memory access
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for context structure, O(n) for simulated memory array
